sources_audited.note: The Census NAICS material supports the six-digit industry definition, freestanding facility scope, specialized equipment and facilities, illustrative center types, and hospital and physician-office cross-references used in the lens, e, and o arguments.

The BLS May 2023 NAICS 621400 table reports the cited employment shares: 41.13% healthcare practitioners and technical occupations, 15.39% registered nurses, 11.19% healthcare support, and 15.71% office and administrative support.
The AMA 2026 survey supports the cited 72% incorporation rate, documentation and summarization use cases, privacy and safety-validation requirements, liability priority, and 27% no-training figure.
Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or take public their business within five years in its fall 2024 survey.

GAO documents physician-practice acquisition and roll-up structures and finds commercial price effects that vary by consolidation type and specialty, including evidence of increases after some private-equity acquisitions.
The BLS industry projections table reports broader NAICS 621400 real output of $217.8 billion in 2024 and $307.4 billion in 2034, with 3.5% annual growth.

MedPAC supports the cited ASC count, for-profit and physician-ownership discussion, five-entity ownership-stake trend, facility growth, FFS service-volume growth, site-of-care context, and absence of required national ASC cost reporting.
CMS supports the 2.6% CY 2026 ASC rate update, affected facility count, IPO-list phaseout, additions to the ASC covered-procedure list, and continued study of outpatient surgical migration.

caveats: The weakest-link labor factor rests on a four-digit employment-count mix rather than a six-digit payroll-weighted task study; the packet discloses this bridge and includes a broad range, but a and rho remain judgment-heavy proxies.

The AMA survey measures physician-reported use and expectations across specialties and settings, not production deployment, avoided labor hours, or LMM-center implementation; it supports direction more strongly than the numeric rho base.

Most ownership, supply, utilization, payment, and site-of-care evidence is specific to Medicare-participating ASCs, while the registered lens also includes urgent care and freestanding emergency and trauma centers with different labor, payer, regulation, and demand economics.

The s5 base is anchored to cross-industry owner intentions rather than completed qualifying control transfers; MedPAC's ownership-stake trend demonstrates an acquisition channel but not a transaction-rate denominator.

The q range has no direct retained-benefit evidence for AI adoption at independent centers, and the GAO consolidation evidence concerns physician practices rather than center-level AI savings; the packet appropriately labels q PROXY and discloses this limitation.

The d5 evidence combines ASC utilization and facility growth with a broader-industry real-output projection, so it cannot cleanly distinguish underlying six-digit quantity growth from price, mix, or migration among sites of care.
summary: Mechanics pass and all eight primitive-cited sources are reachable and support the factual claims attributed to them. The record is useful and candid, with PROXY bridges, ranges, and caveats that disclose the main population and construct mismatches. The largest uncertainties concern the labor-opportunity weakest link, extension of ASC evidence to urgent and emergency centers, completed transfer rates, retained AI benefit, and demand measurement. None is an undisclosed or clearly unsupported defect whose correction or honest evidence-state downgrade would change the LOW_PRIORITY base tier or the already MODEL_CONDITIONED / VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.
